name: Shoyu Ramen with Chicken
steps: Simmer the broth with the soy sauce.
Combine in bowls with a soft-boiled egg.

ingredients_full.note: sliced - thigh stays juicier through the broth than breast

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Simmer the broth with the soy sauce, ginger, and garlic for 15-20 minutes.
Cook the chicken in a separate pan, 6-7 minutes, until cooked through, then slice.
Cook the ramen noodles per package directions, usually 2-3 minutes in boiling water.
Divide the noodles into bowls, ladle the hot broth over, finish with a few drops of sesame oil, and top with sliced chicken, scallions, and a halved soft-boiled egg.
description: Real ramen broth is built on dashi (kombu seaweed and katsuobushi bonito flakes) as its base, layered with a soy-based tare and often a bone stock simmered for hours. Kombu and real bonito flakes aren't distinct items in this database - the canned bonito here is packed in oil or tomato sauce, a different product entirely from the dried, shaved flakes dashi needs. Plain chicken broth gives real savoriness but not dashi's specific depth; there's no honest substitute for it here.

name: Miso Ramen with Pork
steps: Simmer the broth, whisk in the miso.

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Simmer the broth with the ginger and garlic for 10 minutes.
Whisk in the miso paste off the heat once the broth is hot but not boiling hard.
Cook the pork in a separate pan, 5-6 minutes, until browned and cooked through.
Cook the noodles per package directions.
Divide the noodles into bowls, ladle the broth over, and top with the pork, bok choy, corn, and a drizzle of sesame oil.
description: Like shoyu ramen, real miso ramen wants a dashi-based broth underneath the miso - dashi's ingredients aren't distinct items here, so this misses that particular depth. Added corn, a genuinely traditional Sapporo-style miso ramen topping that was missing.

name: Chicken Katsu with Rice

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps: Bread the chicken and pan-fry, 4-5 minutes per side.
Serve over the rice with the ketchup.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps_detailed: Dredge the chicken in flour, dip in egg, then press firmly into the panko.
Heat the oil to 340-350F and fry 3-4 minutes per side until deeply golden and cooked through.
Drain on a rack.
Whisk the ketchup, Worcestershire, and sugar for the sauce.
Cook the sushi rice in 0.9 cup water, covered, 16-18 minutes.
Slice the chicken and serve over the rice with the sauce.
description: Swapped a garlic-herb breadcrumb blend for plain panko, which fries up light and crisp with a neutral flavor - a pre-seasoned crumb changes what katsu is supposed to taste like. Also swapped basmati for real short-grain sushi rice.

name: Salmon Teriyaki Bowl

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps: Glaze with the soy sauce and honey.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps_detailed: Whisk the soy sauce, mirin, honey, ginger, and garlic together.
Pan-sear the salmon skin-side down first if skin-on, 4-5 minutes, then flip and cook 2-3 more minutes.
Pour the sauce into the pan and let it reduce and thicken around the fish, 2-3 minutes, spooning it over as it glazes.
Cook the sushi rice in 0.9 cup water, covered, 16-18 minutes, and serve the glazed salmon over it.
description: Real teriyaki is a glaze of soy sauce, mirin, and sugar reduced until glossy - the shine (teri) is where the name comes from. Added mirin, which the previous version lacked, and swapped basmati for real sushi rice.

name: Steamed Rice & Nori

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps: Top with the nori and soy sauce.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps_detailed: Cook the sushi rice in 1.2 cups water, covered, 16-18 minutes, until tender and slightly sticky.
Toast the nori briefly over a flame or in a dry pan until fragrant and crisp, then cut into strips.
Serve the rice topped with the nori strips, sesame seeds, and a little soy sauce.
description: Swapped basmati for real short-grain sushi rice, the type that actually belongs under nori and next to soy sauce.

name: Japanese Curry Chicken

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps: Brown the chicken with the onion.
Add the curry powder, vegetables, and water, and simmer 20-25 minutes.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps_detailed: Deeply caramelize the onion in the butter, 12-15 minutes, until soft and golden-brown.
Brown the chicken with the onion, 6-7 minutes.
Stir in the flour and curry powder, cooking 2 minutes to form a roux.
Add the carrot, potato, honey, and 2 cups water, and simmer 20-25 minutes until the vegetables are tender and the sauce has thickened.
Cook the sushi rice separately and serve the curry over it.
description: Kare raisu is its own distinct dish, not simply curry powder in gravy - it's built on a roux (butter and flour cooked together, reflecting how curry entered Japan through the British navy in the 19th century) and typically carries a touch of sweetness from grated apple or honey. Swapped basmati for real sushi rice.

name: Chicken & Miso Rice Jar

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps: Layer everything in a jar with the miso dressing.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice
McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Whisk the miso with the sesame oil and a splash of warm water until smooth.
Layer the rice, chicken, and carrot in a jar, tossed with the miso dressing.
description: Swapped basmati for real sushi rice, and worked real miso into the dressing rather than plain soy sauce, matching the jar's own name.

name: Beef Yakisoba
steps_detailed: Cook the noodles per package directions, then drain and set aside.
Heat oil in a wok over high heat. Sear the beef 1-2 minutes per side; remove.
Stir-fry the garlic, cabbage, and carrot 2-3 minutes until tender-crisp.
Add the noodles and beef back in, along with the soy sauce, ketchup, and Worcestershire, tossing everything together over high heat 1-2 minutes.
description: Real yakisoba noodles are a specific pre-steamed wheat noodle, different from ramen noodles, which aren't a distinct item here - ramen noodles are a reasonable stand-in shape-wise, though the texture differs slightly. Yakisoba sauce is also its own distinct condiment, closer to a Japanese take on Worcestershire, sweetened and thickened - ketchup and Worcestershire together get much closer to it than plain soy sauce, which the previous version used alone.

name: Miso Glazed Salmon
steps: Broil the salmon with the glaze, 8-10 minutes.
steps_detailed: Whisk the miso, honey, mirin, and soy sauce together.
Broil or pan-sear the salmon, brushing with the glaze, 8-10 minutes, until just cooked through and caramelized at the edges.
description: Saikyo-yaki style miso salmon, built on real miso and mirin.

name: Yakitori Chicken

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps: Glaze the chicken with the soy sauce and honey.

ingredients_full.note: cubed, skin-on if available - the fat rendering over the fire is a real part of yakitori's flavor

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps_detailed: Thread the chicken onto skewers if available.
Simmer the soy sauce, mirin, and honey together in a small pan 3-4 minutes until reduced into a glossy glaze.
Grill or broil the chicken, basting with the glaze and turning every couple of minutes, 10-12 minutes total, until charred at the edges and cooked through.
Cook the sushi rice separately and serve alongside.
description: Grilled skewered chicken basted in a soy-mirin tare glaze. Real yakitori tare is often a much older sauce built up over years at a single restaurant, which obviously can't be replicated - the basic soy-mirin-sweetener reduction here is the same underlying technique. Swapped jasmine for real sushi rice.

name: Tonkatsu

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps: Bread the pork and pan-fry, 4-5 minutes per side.
Serve over the rice with the ketchup.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps_detailed: Dredge the pork in flour, dip in the beaten egg, then press firmly into the panko so it adheres in a thick, even layer.
Heat the oil in a heavy pan to 340-350F. Fry the pork 3-4 minutes per side, until deeply golden and cooked through.
Drain on a rack, not paper towels.
Whisk the ketchup, Worcestershire, and sugar together for the sauce.
Cook the sushi rice in 0.9 cup water, covered, 16-18 minutes.
Slice the cutlet into strips and serve over the rice with the sauce.
description: Real tonkatsu is deep-fried, not baked - the previous recipe's own name honestly flagged that compromise. The crust's shattering crunch only happens in actual hot oil. Real tonkatsu sauce (tonkatsu sosu) is its own distinct bottled product; Worcestershire sauce plus ketchup and a little sugar is a genuinely common, reasonable home approximation for it.

name: Gyudon (Beef Rice Bowl)

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps: Simmer the onion with the soy sauce.

ingredients_full.note: sliced very thin against the grain

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps_detailed: Simmer the onion in 1/2 cup water with the soy sauce, mirin, and sugar for 5 minutes until softened.
Add the beef and simmer 3-4 minutes, just until cooked through.
Cook the sushi rice in 0.9 cup water, covered, 16-18 minutes.
Spoon the beef, onion, and sauce over the rice, topped with a soft-boiled egg or raw yolk if using.
description: Thin-sliced beef and onion simmered in a soy-mirin liquid over rice. Swapped jasmine rice for real short-grain sushi rice, since Japanese rice cooking wants a shorter, stickier grain.

name: Tofu Poke-Style Bowl

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli
365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps_detailed: Marinate the tofu in the soy sauce and sesame oil for at least 10 minutes.
Toss with the cucumber and serve over the sushi rice, topped with sesame seeds.
description: Poke itself is Hawaiian, not Japanese, though it draws directly on Japanese technique brought by Japanese immigrants to Hawaii - the soy-sesame dressing here is essentially shoyu poke's base. Swapped jasmine for real sushi rice.

name: Onigiri (Rice Balls)

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

steps: Shape the warm rice around the fish filling into a triangle.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Short Grain White Sushi Rice

ingredients_full.note: flaked, standing in for a salted salmon or bonito filling

steps_detailed: Cook the sushi rice in 1.3 cups water, covered, 16-18 minutes.
Wet your hands to keep the rice from sticking, take a palmful of warm rice, press a small well into the center, tuck in a little of the flaked fish, and fold the rice back over to seal it inside.
Shape into a triangle by cupping your hands and pressing the corners.
Wrap a strip of nori around the base and finish with a scatter of sesame seeds.
description: Onigiri are usually built around a filling - classically salted salmon, pickled plum, or bonito flakes tucked into the center - and the previous version had no filling at all, closer to a plain rice side than actual onigiri. Pickled herring stands in for the traditional salty fish filling; it's an honest, imperfect substitute, since real salted salmon or umeboshi aren't distinct items here.

name: Tamagoyaki (Rolled Omelette)
steps: Whisk the eggs with the soy sauce and honey.
Cook in thin layers, rolling each into the last.
steps_detailed: Whisk the eggs with the soy sauce, honey, and mirin until smooth and well combined.
Heat a lightly oiled nonstick or tamagoyaki pan over medium-low heat. Pour in a thin layer of egg, just enough to coat the bottom.
As it just barely sets, roll it up from one side to the other with chopsticks or a spatula.
Push the roll to one side, oil the empty part of the pan, add another thin layer of egg, letting it flow slightly under the existing roll, and roll again.
Repeat until all the egg is used, forming a multi-layered log.
Slice into pieces to serve.
description: A rolled, layered sweet-savory omelette. Real dashimaki tamago works dashi directly into the egg for savory depth alongside the sweetness - dashi isn't achievable with what's in this database, so this stays a plainer, sweeter-leaning version.

name: Edamame Hummus with Nori Chips

ingredients.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps: Blend the edamame with the sesame oil and lemon juice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Blend the edamame with the sesame oil, lemon juice, and garlic until smooth.
Toast the nori pieces briefly in a dry pan or over a flame until crisp and fragrant.
Serve the hummus with the nori chips for scooping.
description: A modern, Western-fusion snack rather than a traditional Japanese dish, though it draws on real Japanese ingredients - edamame, nori, sesame oil - in a reasonable way.

name: Wasabi Roasted Almonds
steps: Toss the almonds with the wasabi and soy sauce.
steps_detailed: Toss the almonds with the wasabi and soy sauce.
Roast at 350F for 8-10 minutes.
description: A genuine Japanese snack style - wasabi and soy together on roasted nuts is a real, established combination.

name: Matcha Mochi Bites
steps: Whisk into a batter and microwave in bursts until thick and stretchy.

ingredients_full.note: ground into flour, or use store-bought mochiko/sweet rice flour

steps_detailed: Whisk the glutinous rice flour, matcha, sugar, and milk together into a smooth batter.
Microwave in a covered, greased dish in 1-minute bursts, stirring between each, 3-4 minutes total, until it turns from a runny batter into a thick, translucent, sticky mass.
Let cool just enough to handle, then dust your hands with a little extra rice flour and pinch off small pieces, rolling in the grated coconut to coat.
description: Mochi is defined by glutinous rice flour's specific starch, which creates its stretchy, chewy texture. The previous version used coconut flour and sweetened condensed milk, which has nothing to do with real mochi at all - this wasn't a fair-substitute judgment call, it was simply the wrong ingredient for the dish's whole identity. Fixed with real glutinous rice flour.

name: Ponzu Dipping Sauce
steps_detailed: Whisk the soy sauce, lime juice, and rice vinegar together.
description: Real ponzu is traditionally made with yuzu, a specific Japanese citrus, not lime - yuzu isn't a distinct item in this database, and lime is genuinely different (brighter, more purely sour, missing yuzu's floral complexity), so this is a lime-soy sauce in ponzu's style rather than real ponzu.

name: Sesame Miso Dressing

ingredients.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

ingredients_full.ingredient: McCormick Sesame Oil, Aceite De Atonjoli

steps_detailed: Whisk the miso, sesame oil, rice vinegar, and honey together.
description: A genuine, common Japanese dressing style built on real miso and sesame oil.

name: Miso Soup with Tofu
steps: Whisk the miso into hot water.
steps_detailed: Bring 1.5 cups water to a bare simmer with the fish sauce.
Whisk in the miso paste once the water is hot but not boiling hard.
Add the tofu and warm through gently, 2 minutes, without boiling.
Top with the scallions.
description: Real miso soup is built on a dashi base - dashi isn't achievable with what's in this database. A small amount of fish sauce adds a little of the savory depth dashi would normally provide, but it's a genuinely different flavor and doesn't replicate dashi's clean, delicate character.
